AuditLog.title: AuditLog
AuditLog.description: Represents an audit log entry for changes made in the system.

id.description: Unique identifier for the audit log entry.

userId.description: Reference to the user who made the change. (Relationship: User 1:N AuditLog)

timestamp.description: The timestamp of when the change was made.

entityType.description: The type of entity that was changed (e.g., 'Product', 'Order').

entityId.description: The ID of the entity that was changed.

changeType.description: The type of change that was made (e.g., 'Create', 'Update', 'Delete').

oldValue.description: The value of the field before the change.

newValue.description: The value of the field after the change.

Report.title: Report
Report.description: Represents a report generated by the system.

dateGenerated.description: The date and time the report was generated.

generatedByUserId.description: Reference to the user who generated the report. (Relationship: User 1:N Report)

reportData.description: The summarized content of the operational report.

Partner.title: Partner
Partner.description: Represents a partner, which can be a client, supplier, etc.

definition.description: Stores audit logs for each user.  Path-based ownership: all audit logs belong to the user specified in the path. This design achieves Authorization Independence.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique identifier for the user.

params.name: auditLogId
params.description: The unique identifier for the audit log entry.

definition.description: Stores reports generated by each user. Path-based ownership simplifies security rules. This design achieves Authorization Independence.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique identifier for the user.

params.name: reportId
params.description: The unique identifier for the report.

params.name: partnerId
params.description: The unique identifier for the partner.

firestore.reasoning: The Firestore structure is designed to ensure secure and scalable data management for the SeniorComex application. It leverages path-based ownership and denormalization to simplify security rules and enable atomic operations.  The structure includes collections for users, audit logs, and reports. User data is stored in a dedicated collection. Audit logs are stored as subcollections under each user document (`/users/{userId}/auditLogs/{auditLogId}`), ensuring clear ownership and simplifying access control. This structure supports the requirement of logging all changes, including who made them and when, by providing a complete audit trail. Reports are also structured as a subcollection under the user who generated them (`/users/{userId}/reports/{reportId}`), ensuring clear ownership and straightforward access control. This design facilitates role-based access control (Administrator, Operator, Finance, Logistics) as the user's role is stored directly in the user document, which simplifies access control logic in the security rules.  Authorization Independence is achieved by relating AuditLog and Report documents to users using path based ownership (`/users/{userId}/auditLogs/{auditLogId}` and `/users/{userId}/reports/{reportId}`).  This approach avoids the need for `get()` calls to retrieve parent document data during authorization, ensuring atomic operations and easier debugging. QAPs (Rules are not Filters) are supported by the segregation of data into dedicated collections, each with well-defined security requirements, and all documents in a collection share the same security requirements.  This prevents the need for complex filtering logic in the security rules.
